---
title: 'Define: Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)'
date: '2023-09-24T21:42:00.0050000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: 'The Freedom Center'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '5391123851876555296'
blogger_filename: '/2023/09/define-attention-deficithyperactivity.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/The Freedom Center/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)'
---

<p>&nbsp;</p><h2>Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD)</h2>
<p>Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) is a neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by persistent patterns of inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity that interfere with daily functioning or development. The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition (DSM-5) is commonly used to diagnose ADHD.</p>
<p>The primary diagnostic criteria for ADHD according to the DSM-5 are:</p>
<h3>Inattention</h3>
<p>Six or more symptoms of inattention for children up to age 16 years, or five or more for adolescents aged 17 years and older and adults; symptoms must be present for at least 6 months:</p>
<ol>
<li>Fails to give close attention to details or makes careless mistakes.</li>
<li>Has difficulty sustaining attention in tasks or play.</li>
<li>Does not seem to listen when spoken to directly.</li>
<li>Does not follow through on instructions and fails to finish tasks.</li>
<li>Has difficulty organizing tasks and activities.</li>
<li>Avoids, dislikes, or is reluctant to engage in tasks requiring sustained mental effort.</li>
<li>Loses things necessary for tasks and activities.</li>
<li>Is easily distracted by extraneous stimuli.</li>
<li>Is forgetful in daily activities.</li>
</ol>
<h3>Hyperactivity-Impulsivity</h3>
<p>Six or more symptoms of hyperactivity-impulsivity for children up to age 16 years, or five or more for adolescents aged 17 years and older and adults; symptoms must be present for at least 6 months:</p>
<ol>
<li>Fidgets with or taps hands or feet or squirms in seat.</li>
<li>Leaves seat in situations where remaining seated is expected.</li>
<li>Runs about or climbs in situations where it’s inappropriate.</li>
<li>Unable to play or engage in activities quietly.</li>
<li>Is "on the go," acts as if "driven by a motor."</li>
<li>Talks excessively.</li>
<li>Blurts out an answer before a question has been completed.</li>
<li>Has difficulty waiting his or her turn.</li>
<li>Interrupts or intrudes on others.</li>
</ol>
<h3>Additional Criteria</h3>
<ul>
<li>Several symptoms must be present before age 12.</li>
<li>Symptoms must be present in at least two settings (e.g., home, school, work).</li>
<li>There must be clear evidence that the symptoms interfere with social, academic, or occupational functioning.</li>
</ul>
<h3><strong>Reasons for Late Diagnosis:</strong></h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Coping Mechanisms</strong>: Some adults develop coping strategies that help them manage ADHD symptoms, thereby masking the disorder's more obvious indicators.</li>
<li><strong>Subtle Symptoms</strong>: In adults, symptoms might be less overt and could manifest as issues like chronic disorganization, poor time management, or emotional dysregulation, rather than hyperactivity.</li>
<li><strong>Awareness</strong>: Earlier generations were raised with less awareness and understanding of ADHD, which could have contributed to a missed diagnosis in childhood.</li>
<li><strong>High Functioning</strong>: Individuals who are intellectually gifted or have strong compensatory skills may perform adequately in academic or work environments, obscuring the need for a diagnosis.</li>
<li><strong>Stigma</strong>: Societal stigma about ADHD and mental health conditions can deter people from seeking a diagnosis.</li>
<li><strong>Comorbid Conditions</strong>: ADHD often occurs alongside other psychiatric conditions like depression or anxiety, which could be diagnosed first, overshadowing the ADHD.</li>
<li><strong>Gender Bias</strong>: ADHD has historically been diagnosed more frequently in males, especially those who display hyperactivity. As a result, females or individuals who display primarily inattentive symptoms are often overlooked.</li>
</ol>
<h3><strong>Implications:</strong></h3>
<ol>
<li><strong>Emotional Impact</strong>: A late diagnosis can be both a relief and a source of anxiety or confusion, as adults grapple with reinterpreting their life experiences through the lens of ADHD.</li>
<li><strong>Treatment</strong>: Medication and behavioral therapies effective in children are generally effective in adults as well, although adult-focused resources may be less plentiful.</li>
<li><strong>Personal and Professional Life</strong>: Understanding one has ADHD can be a pivotal moment for personal development, potentially leading to more effective coping strategies and better outcomes in work and relationships.</li>
</ol>
<h3><strong>Weight of Evidence:</strong></h3>
<p>Research increasingly supports the idea that ADHD is not just a childhood disorder but a lifelong condition. Late-diagnosed adults are gaining more attention in clinical and academic circles, emphasizing the importance of diagnosis and treatment options suitable for this age group.</p>
